coredata.dc:title: Two-photon excitation triggers combined chemo-photothermal therapy via doped carbon nanohybrid dots for effective breast cancer treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Ardekani, Sara Madadi
Dehghani, Alireza
Hassan, Mahbub
Kianinia, Mehran
Aharonovich, Igor
Gomes, Vincent G.
coredata.dc:description: Remotely triggered drug delivery using nanoparticles is an area of great interest for targeted therapy to fight cancer. In this work, we synthesized photoresponsive nanoparticles to remotely initiate the delivery of doxorubicin (DOX) to 3D cultured human breast cancer cells (MCF-7) via NIR two-photon excitation (TPE) using nitrogen-doped and surface passivated (PEG200) carbon nanohybrid dots (CNDs). On-demand drug delivery relies on bio-compatible, photo-responsive nano-carriers with high quantum yields. A facile (5min synthesis) one-pot hot plate method was used to synthesize functionalized and surface passivated carbon nanohybrid dots (CND-P) having 53% quantum yield (QY). Compared to CNDs prepared from citric acid (CND-C) and citric acid plus urea (CND-N), CND-P had QY enhanced by factors of 12.6 and 4.4, respectively. The up-converted emission intensity of CND-P was strengthened by a factor of 4.5 over that of CND-N from nitrogen doped CNDs for similar test conditions (wavelength, excitation power and concentration). The drug loading capacity of CND-P was measured to be 0.98w/w with the ability to release DOX via two-photon excitation (TPE). Intense green luminescence was observed under both 360 and 780nm lasers using single and two-photon excitations. The highly biocompatible CND-P showed 88% cell viability at concentrations as high as 1100µg/mL. The combined chemo- and photothermal therapeutic effect of the DOX-loaded CND-P (CND-P@DOX) complex resulted in the death of 78% of the MCF-7 cells compared to 59% with DOX alone.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanohybrid dots
Two-photon excitation
Bioimaging
Breast cancer
Chemotherapy
Photothermal therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: One pot synthesis of nitrogen doped and surface passivated CNDs
Loading and release profiles of DOX
Cytotoxicity, imaging and evaluation of cellular uptake
Ex-vivo drug release and bioimaging of 3D cultured MCF-7 cells
Cytotoxicity and two-photon 2D and 3D z-stack bioimaging
Drug loading, pH and two-photon triggered drug release with CND-P
Multifunctional CND-P for two-photon mediated chemo-photothermal therapy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CND-P (scale: 20nm), (B) FFT with no visible diffraction, (C) Particle size distribution of CND-P.
FTIR spectra of (A) CND-C, (B) CND-N and (C) CND-P.
XPS spectra of CND-P: (A) full survey spectrum, (B) C1s spectrum, (C) N1s spectrum, and (D) O1s spectrum (please refer to ESI Fig. S3 for details on XPS spectra of CND-C and CND-N).
(A) UV–Vis spectra of CND-C, CND-N, and CND-P (inset left to right: photograph of CND-C, CND-N, and CND-P under 365nm excitation), (B) PL spectra of CND-P, (C) Lifetime decay profile of CND-P.
Comparison of up-conversion intensities of (A) CND-P and (B) CND-N at 780nm excitation wavelength (35mW power).
Cell viability of MCF-7 as a function of CND-N and CND-P concentrations after 24h.
Two-photon imaging with 780nm (35mW) pulsed laser: (A) Nuclei stained with DAPI, (B) CND-P in cytoplasm and nuclei, (C) Bright-field image, (D) Overlay of A and B (scale bar: 20μm).

Comparison of 2D (A–D) and 3D (E–H) z-stack (30µm) images of 3D cultured MCF-7 cells cultivated with CND-P (500µg/mL): (A) Nuclei stained with DAPI (red), (B) CND-P internalized by cells (green), (C) overlay of A and B, (D) Bright-field image of cells, (E) 3D reconstruct of cell nuclei stained with DAPI, (F) CND-P internalized by the cell cytoplasm and nucleolus rather than remaining on the surface of cells, (G) overlay of E and F, (H) Bright-field image of 3D cultured cells (scale bar: 40µm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Absorption spectra of pure DOX and CND-P; (B) PL emission spectra of pure DOX and CND-P; (C) PL emission spectra of CND-P@DOX complex before TPE and PL emission spectra of released DOX after TPE (780nm, 35mW laser) over 25min followed by centrifugation;; (D) Drug release vs irradiation time for CND-P@DOX complex after TPE and centrifugation (780nm, 35mW laser) and CND-P@DOX complex after centrifugation without irradiation.

(A) Photothermal profiles for control (water) and aqueous dispersions of CND-P at 30, 40 and 50µg/mL with 780nm 35mW TPE in a 10mm cuvette and 1ml volume, (B) Cell viability as a function of DOX concentration.
Cell viability tests with control (MCF-7 cells), CND-P, DOX and CND-P@DOX (concentrations of DOX and CND-P were 1000nM and 50μg/mL, respectively).

3D reconstruct of the colocalization of CND-P and DOX inside the MCF-7 cells. The panels from left to right show CND-P (green), DOX (red), overlay of the two and Bright field image (scale bar: 20µm) (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

CLSM observations of drug release from CND-P@DOX in MCF-7 cells after 5 (A–D), 15 (E-H) and 25 (I–L) min TPE (780nm, 35mW). CND-P fluorescence in cells (green), DOX fluorescence in cells (red), brightfield, and overlays of the three images (scale bar: 20µm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram of the synthesis procedure of biocompatible CND-P for multifunctional on-demand drug release and combined chemo-photothermal therapy via two-photon excitation.
Comparison of properties for the synthesized CNDs.

head.ce:title: Two-photon excitation triggers combined chemo-photothermal therapy via doped carbon nanohybrid dots for effective breast cancer treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The University of Sydney, School of Chemical & Biomolecular Engineering, NSW 2006, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The University of Technology Sydney, School of Mathematical & Physical Sciences, NSW 2007, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Remotely triggered drug delivery using nanoparticles is an area of great interest for targeted therapy to fight cancer. In this work, we synthesized photoresponsive nanoparticles to remotely initiate the delivery of doxorubicin (DOX) to 3D cultured human breast cancer cells (MCF-7) via NIR two-photon excitation (TPE) using nitrogen-doped and surface passivated (PEG200) carbon nanohybrid dots (CNDs). On-demand drug delivery relies on bio-compatible, photo-responsive nano-carriers with high quantum yields. A facile (5min synthesis) one-pot hot plate method was used to synthesize functionalized and surface passivated carbon nanohybrid dots (CND-P) having 53% quantum yield (QY). Compared to CNDs prepared from citric acid (CND-C) and citric acid plus urea (CND-N), CND-P had QY enhanced by factors of 12.6 and 4.4, respectively. The up-converted emission intensity of CND-P was strengthened by a factor of 4.5 over that of CND-N from nitrogen doped CNDs for similar test conditions (wavelength, excitation power and concentration). The drug loading capacity of CND-P was measured to be 0.98w/w with the ability to release DOX via two-photon excitation (TPE). Intense green luminescence was observed under both 360 and 780nm lasers using single and two-photon excitations. The highly biocompatible CND-P showed 88% cell viability at concentrations as high as 1100µg/mL. The combined chemo- and photothermal therapeutic effect of the DOX-loaded CND-P (CND-P@DOX) complex resulted in the death of 78% of the MCF-7 cells compared to 59% with DOX alone.

ce:section.ce:para: The emerging field of nanomedicine seeks to revolutionize medical diagnostics and therapy through the development of multifunctional nanoplatforms [1,2]. Recent advances in engineering carbon quantum dot (CQD) probes and the promising benefits of this technology have stimulated a shift in focus from the synthesis of single-component probes towards the design of complex nanostructures composed of multifunctional targeting, imaging, and therapeutic modes. For example, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) integrated with MRI contrast agents or radionuclides may be used for dual-mode imaging [3], whereas combined with drugs or nucleic acid therapeutics where CQDs can serve as traceable delivery vehicles [4]. More importantly, designing CQD-based multifunctional architectures require systematic analysis and formulation so that each functionality can perform independently and does not create cross perturbation with other functionalities. Photo-theranostic or the application of light in diagnostic imaging and therapy of cancer has been shown to be promising for multimodal cancer cell imaging and therapy [5]. CQDs have emerged as an ideal platform in this regard since their intrinsic aromatic and π-conjugated chemical bonds possess excellent photothermal conversion ability and thus can absorb and efficiently convert NIR light into heat.

Spatiotemporal controllability and clinical relevance of light make CQDs an excellent choice for triggered drug release via external stimuli. Due to strong absorption of light by hemoglobin, myoglobin, and melanin in the UV and near-UV range, these wavelengths are not suitable as external stimuli. However, NIR (650–900 nm) radiation can safely penetrate deep (cm scale) inside tissues without causing any photoresponsive chemical damage [6,7]. To harness the advantages of high-quality imaging of UV–visible light with the high penetration depth of NIR, the conversion of NIR to a safe, visible range via up-conversion can be used. Previously, drug release from photoresponsive nanoparticles such as plasmons [8], liposome [9], polymer [10], nano gel [11], solid organic [12], mesoporous silica [13] and upconverting nanoparticles [14] have been reported.

Static two-dimensional (2D) cell culture systems have been used widely for in vitro studies of preclinical therapeutic developments in several types of cancer. However, these 2D models cannot provide the complex microenvironment in native tissue including cell-cell interactions. Hence, the relative resistance of tumor cells to chemotherapy drugs cannot be accurately modeled. Cellular microenvironment can be better mimicked using rotary three-dimensional (3D) cell culture systems [15]. Thus, the proliferation of cancer cells and their response to the treatment can be better modeled in a dynamic 3D microenvironment. As previously shown, MCF-7 human breast cancer cells cultured in a 3D cell culturing environment is closer to the in vivo tumors compared to conventional 2D cultured cells [16]. Drug delivery offers the ability to transport the drug to the tumor site, thereby avoiding the negative side effects of high dosage and non-targeted delivery. To obtain optimum results, it is important to develop drug delivery systems with controllable/on-demand release properties [17]. For cancer imaging and drug delivery, various types of nanomaterials, such as gold [18], silica [19,20] and iron oxide [21] nanoparticles have been used.

Previous studies on generating carbon dots from citric acid investigated ways to improve the quantum yield (QY) and photoluminescence mainly with amorphous structures containing blends of citric acid (as the main carbon core) with various amine molecules as nitrogen donors [22–28]. Several types of polymers such as PEG and PEI with different molecular weights have been used for surface passivation to increase the quantum yield and lower the toxicity of the nanoparticles [28–31]. However, synthesis via conventional hydrothermal methods by typically using autoclaves for 12–24h to obtain multicomponent carbon dots, result in structures having about 50% QY and produce blue emissions with 360nm excitation [28,31]. Synthesis via pyrolysis using citric acid usually produces carbon dots with QY of less than 10% and significantly weak blue emissions [28,32]. Recent work has shown that nanostructures based on carbon and associated atoms often qualify as “polymer dots” because of their polymeric nature and attributes [33,34]. These studies have opened up an exciting class of subgroup of carbon-related nanoparticles having unique tunable properties.

Our synthesis method was developed on taking into account the key process variables of time, temperature, doping and passivation to obtain enhanced nanostructures for theranostic applications. Here, we present functionalized and surface passivated carbon nanohybrid dots (CNDs) via a facile one-pot synthesis procedure using doped citric acid. Our pyrolysis process is based on a rapid hot-plate method to generate CNDs having high QY (>50%), intense photoluminescence (green emission) and excellent biocompatibility compared to previous studies [25,35–38,66].

We demonstrate a strategy for the first time using two-photon NIR light to trigger the efficient release of an anticancer drug from nitrogen doped (urea) and surface passivated (PEG200) CNDs. Our dual mode strategy enabled us to rapidly synthesize bright biocompatible CND-P as a photoresponsive nanoparticle to investigate bioimaging and on-demand drug release behavior using NIR two-photon excitation (TPE). We tested the effects of combined chemo- and photothermal therapy using the synthesized CND-P (Scheme 1 ) on MCF-7 breast cancer cells cultured via the 3D Rotary Cell Culture System (RCCS).

Reagents such as citric acid, urea, and poly(ethylene) glycol (PEG) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich (Australia). Dialysis membranes were purchased from Spectra/Pro Biotech (Australia), and MCF-7 breast cancer cell was procured from ATCC (Australia).

ce:section.ce:section-title: One pot synthesis of nitrogen doped and surface passivated CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: 2.1g citric acid, 1.8g urea and 2g PEG (MW∼200) were added to a beaker and heated on a hotplate to 180°C for 5min. The final product was collected by adding deionized (DI) water and centrifuged at 6000rpm for 30min. The dark green solution was removed and passed through a cellulose acetate filter (pore size 0.45 µm), followed by ultrafiltration via dialysis tube (MWCO 30kDa) for 30min at 5000g. The stock solution with a quantitative yield of 59% and quantum yield of 53% was lyophilized, denoted as CND-P and kept in a desiccator for further analysis. Samples without N-doping (CND-C) and without surface passivation (CND-N) were also prepared for comparison (see ESI for synthesis details).

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) is the ratio of photons absorbed to the photons emitted through fluorescence. Quinine sulfate (QY=54%) was chosen as the standard reference sample. The QY of all samples in water were estimated with a 10 mm fluorescence cuvette according to:
QY ( X ) =
QY
standard
A
standard
A
X
F
X
F
standard
η
X
η
standard
2
where A is absorbance at the excitation wavelength, F is integrated emission under fluorescence spectrum, η is the refractive index of the solvent and subscript ‘X’ denotes the sample. Absorption threshold was set below 0.10 to minimize any re-absorption effect.

To examine the size and size distribution of carbon nanomaterials, a transmission electron microscope (JEOL 2100, USA) operating at 200kV was used. The synthesized carbon nanomaterials were deposited on Cu grids, coated with a carbon film (200 mesh) and dried in a vacuum desiccator for two days. The Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer (ThermoFisher, USA) was used to obtain the percent transmission and surface state data for the samples. The Cary60 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA) was used to record UV–visible absorption spectra. Fluorescence spectra with variable excitation wavelengths were obtained with a CLARIOstar spectrometer (BMG LABTECH, USA). The PL lifetime decay profile was measured using a customized confocal microscope (diode laser PiLas with 80ps linewidth and 40MHz repetition rate) setup with a 412nm pulsed laser for excitation. To evaluate the two-photon absorption and emission behavior, a confocal microscope with pulsed laser was used. ζ-potential (Zetasizer-3000, Malvern Instruments, UK) measurements were performed to determine the surface charge of the samples. To investigate the chemical composition of the CNDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed (Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi USA) using a monochromatic Al-K X-ray source (h=1486.68eV).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading and release profiles of DOX
ce:section.ce:para: To characterize loading and release profiles, DOX was mixed with CND-P in DI water and subjected to overnight stirring in the dark. The unbound DOX was removed by centrifugation using Amicon ultracentrifuge filter tube with a 3kDa molecular cut-off for 10min at 5000g to obtain DOX bound to CND-P (denoted as CND-P@DOX).

The loading capacity for doxorubicin was quantified using the Beer-Lambert law by measuring the absorbance peak of DOX at 488nm [1,2]. The drug loading profile was calculated according to the following equation:
Drug loading ( % ) =
weight of DOX in CND - P @ DOX
weight of DOX in feed
∗ 100

CND-P was dispersed in PBS at a pH of 5.0, 6.0, and 7.4 while being stirred continuously at 37°C. Dialysis was performed with 3kDa molecular weight cut-off to remove the released DOX. The release profiles were measured as a function of time using UV–Vis spectroscopy and were estimated as follows:
Release profile ( % ) =
weight of DOX released from CND - P @ DOX
weight of DOX in CND - P @ DOX
∗ 100

To evaluate on-demand release of DOX, CND-P@DOX was divided into six equal aliquots. One sample was designated as the control sample and the remaining five sets were set aside for two-photon-triggered DOX release experiments. A pulsed laser with a wavelength of 780nm was used as the NIR two-photon excitation source operating at 35 mW. The samples were irradiated for 5, 10, 15, 20 and 25min in five different cuvettes (10 mm quartz cuvette with the volume of 3.0ml). The released DOX was removed by centrifugation using the Amicon ultracentrifuge filter tube with a 3kDa molecular weight cut-off at 5000g. The released DOX amounts were measured by comparing the CND-P@DOX absorption peak at 488nm with the DOX standard curve using a UV–Vis spectrometer. To verify our results, the excitation/emission peak positions of the CND-P, DOX, and CND-P@DOX before and after release were measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity, imaging and evaluation of cellular uptake
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the cytotoxicity of CND-P via (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay, MCF-7 cells were incubated with various concentrations of CND-P and CND-N (20, 100, 300, 500, 700, 900, 1100µg/mL) for 24h with a density of 40k in cells/well. Subsequently, the cytotoxicity was quantified by dissolving the formazan crystals formed by the live cells and by measuring the absorbance at 550nm.

For the cell imaging experiment, breast cancer cells (MCF-7) were seeded in each well of 6-well sterile chamber slides in a 500 μL culture medium at a cell density of 40k cells/well. Following overnight incubation, the media was replaced with a culture media containing 500μg/mL CNDs and incubated for 24h. The culture media were removed from the sterile chambers, and the cells were washed twice with 1.0 mL phosphate buffer solution (PBS). The cells were fixed with 80% ethanol and imaged by a Leica SP5 confocal microscope with mercury lamp as the light source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ex-vivo drug release and bioimaging of 3D cultured MCF-7 cells
ce:section.ce:para: For bioimaging, 3D cell culture was conducted based on a previously reported protocol [39]. Sterilized Synthecon cell culture vessel (10 mL size) was rinsed with pre-warmed culture medium (serum free) under sterile conditions in a laminar flow hood and allowed to incubate overnight in the rinsing solution. The solution was discarded and replaced with the full culture media. The cells were seeded at a density of 5×106 cells/mL (10 mL total volume). The rotation speed was adjusted to 12rpm and increased by 2rpm over every three days to 24rpm as the cells proliferated, and the medium (containing 50μg/mL of CND-P for all experiments except the negative and positive control) was replaced after every three days over a three week period. The cells formed loose aggregates and condensed to form rounded structures. To assess the effect of DOX on the cells, control experiments were conducted. MCF-7 cells were cultured in a 6-well plate at a density of 40k cells/well. After 24h incubation, the culture medium was replaced with the medium with various DOX concentrations (100, 200, 500, 1000 and 2000 nM) and the cells were incubated further for 48h. A cell viability experiment was conducted to determine the suitable concentration of DOX and CND-P@DOX. To evaluate the two-photon light- triggered on-demand drug release behavior and photothermal effect, the final concentration of 1000 nM (0.58μg/ml) of DOX-loaded on CND-P (50μg/mL) was added to each RCCS vessel. For comparison, MCF-7 cells were also cultured for 24h with the pure CND-P and DOX with final concentrations of 50μg/mL and 1μM, respectively. Cell aggregates were irradiated with a 35 mW laser over time (5, 15, 25min). Samples with DOX only (positive control) and cells in culture media (negative control) were also exposed to the same laser irradiation.

The morphology and size distribution of CNDs were characterized using the TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 , the CND-P particles are amorphous and round-shaped with a particle size of about 7–10nm. Contrary to previous reports based on CA and urea precursors [40], the non-crystalline structure of CND-P (no diffractions noted in FFT) can be attributed to our very brief reaction time to form CNDs (5min). The formation of CNDs involves a condensation reaction (H2O elimination) between -OH and -H inter- and intra-molecular species to form a network of conjugated double bonds [41]. CND-P shows a clear increase in the average particle size compared to the non-passivated CNDs. The TEM images and size distribution results for CND-C and CND-N are given in the ESI (Fig. S1&S2). No significant change in size was observed between the CNDs before and after N-doping.

The FTIR characterization of CNDs indicated the presence of specific surface functional groups (Fig. 2 ). The presence of CO, CO, and OH shows the formation of CNDs [42,43]. These modes are clear around 1700 cm−1 wavelength for CO [40], 1195 cm−1 for CO and 3246–3495 cm−1 are attributed to OH in the system [44]. The broad peak between 1390 and 1500 cm−1 has been ascribed to the carboxyl groups. In Fig. 2B, the peaks at 1195 cm−1 and 1400–1500 cm−1 have been assigned to CN bond, which confirms that N-doping was successful [44]. An increase in the stretching vibration of CH bond at 2888 cm−1 and CO bond at 1195 cm−1 can be observed, following the PEGylation process. The presence of OH and CH groups and PEG enable CNDs to be dispersed in water [32].

In the Raman spectra (Fig. S8, ESI), the overlap between the D and G bands and the relative intensities of the disordered D band over the G band indicate the amorphous nature of the CNDs [45]. The XPS spectra (Fig. 3 ) show the chemical composition of CND-P. The deconvolution of the C1s spectra for CND-P (Fig. 3B) shows peaks at 284.8, 286.6 and 288.8eV, which are attributed to CC/CC, CO/CN and CO, respectively. The high-resolution N1s peak of CND-N appears at 400 eV. The spectrum can be fitted with 2 Gaussian peaks at 399.9 and 401.6 eV which correspond to the CN and NH, respectively [46]. An increase in the relative intensity of CO groups is observed after the surface passivation. For the O1s spectra, the CO also show an increase in intensity. The increased fluorescence of CND-P compared to CND-N and also the red shifted emission (520 to 540nm) is caused by the synergistic effect of PEG polymer and CND-N fluorescence centers [46]. During the heating process, PEG forms a network and holds the fluorophores inside and limit the mobilization of fluorophores, restrain vibrations and eventually lead to enhanced emission. The XPS survey (Fig. S3, ESI) of CND-N and CND-C further confirms successful N-doping [40,47].

The optical properties were examined using UV–Vis and PL spectroscopy. Fig. 4 A shows the UV–Vis absorption of CNDs at room temperature. Two notable peaks can be observed in the spectra for CND-N and CND-P. The peak at 255nm is due to the π→π∗ transition of CC. The absorption peaks at around 350 and 400nm stem from CN and CO transitions (both n→π∗), respectively[48]. The effect of surface passivation can be observed from the changes in the peak intensities. Following surface passivation, a decrease in the CN functional groups can be seen from the absorption spectrum of the CND-P, while the CO peak shows a minor increase (also demonstrated by changes in FTIR peak intensities). A notable red shift from blue to green can be observed on progressing from CND-C to CND-N as a result of N-doping. PEGylation of CND-P further shifted the color to brighter green with 365nm excitation. A previous report indicates that by increasing the size of the dots, redshifts may occur for emission from CNDs [49]. However, no change in the average sizes between the CND-C and CND-N was observed from the TEM data (2.4–3.2nm, Fig. S1 & S2 in ESI). It can be concluded that the red shift from 480nm for CND-C to 520nm for CND-N at the same excitation wavelength (390nm) is due to the N-doping of CND-C.

Fig. 4B shows the excitation-independent feature of CND-P. The PL peaks have shifted towards higher wavelengths which are similar to the luminescence obtained with graphene quantum dots [50]. With excitation at 430nm, the emission peak for CND-P occurs at 540nm, which indicates a Stokes shift of 110nm. This is significantly greater than that of the widely used CdSe/ZnS nanocrystals (≈20nm) [51,52]. Surface engineering of CND-N with PEG200 improved the PL intensity by a factor of two compared to that of CND-N (Fig. S5 ESI). As a result of PEGylation, some of the surface energy traps typically become inactive or emissive upon stabilization [53]; thus, surface passivation increased the PL quantum yield of CND-P significantly. The QY of the CNDs were measured to be 4, 14 and 53% for CND-C CND-N and CND-P, respectively (based on quinine sulfate as reference). An increase in the QY values for CND-P indicates that there is a higher probability of the excited state to be deactivated by fluorescence pathways rather than by a different non-radiative mechanism since the QY of CND-P was significantly greater than those of CND-C and CND-N.

The lifetime decay profile of CND-P (Fig. 4C) was fitted satisfactorily with a double-exponential function with lifetimes of τ1=4.48ns and τ2=0.89ns. As shown by the UV–Vis spectra, the two emission centers of CND-P (the carbogenic core and surface defects) resulted in two different types of transitions (π→π∗ and n→π∗), which were confirmed by the two distinct lifetime values [54,55]. The details on lifetime values for CND-C (Fig. S4) and CND-N and their optical properties are presented in the ESI (Fig. S5).

Two-photon active CNDs are suitable for efficient on-demand drug delivery with light as a stimulus due to its spatiotemporal controllability. Apart from the low penetration depth, UV light is absorbed by the hemoglobin, myoglobin, and melanin. However, this problem can be eliminated when the light in the NIR I window (600–900nm) is used. Hence, the ability of CND-P to absorb two photons simultaneously in the NIR I window and emit light at a lower wavelength (i.e., up-conversion) was investigated. The up-conversion spectra of CND-P excited from 740 to 820nm with emission profiles ranging from 400 to 700nm are shown in Fig. 5 . As can be seen from Fig. 5A and B the highest up-converted emission intensity of CND-P is 4.5 times greater than that of CND-N under similar conditions (excitation wavelength 780 nm, 35 mW power and similar concentration). This enhanced performance of CND-P can be attributed to its higher quantum yield. To further investigate the stability of CND-P, a 780 nm laser at higher power (80 mW) was used, and no bleaching was observed after constant irradiation for 80 min (Fig. S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and two-photon 2D and 3D z-stack bioimaging
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the cytotoxicity of CND-N and CND-P, MCF-7 (breast cancer) cells were used and the cell viability with MTT assay was measured at various concentrations of CND-N and CND-P for 24 h, after which the cytotoxicity was quantified by dissolving the formazan crystals formed by the live cells and measuring the absorbance at 550 nm. As shown in Fig. 6 , passivation has rendered the CND-P less toxic (93% viability) at concentrations as high as 1100 µg/mL. As reported previously, the cytotoxicity of CNDs is due to its ability to generate intracellular reactive oxygen (ROS) [29]. The surface passivation (PEG matrix) in close proximity to the CND-P surface can minimize the interactions between CND-P surface and the cells and prevent ROS generation, thereby rendering the CND-P as less toxic [29,56]. In particular, significantly lower cytotoxicity was observed for CND-P compared to CND-N at concentrations of 300 µg/mL and higher. Table 1 summarizes the enhancements introduced by surface engineering via N-doping and surface passivation of CNDs.

To evaluate the potential of CND-P in 2D and 3D cell imaging, an in vitro bioimaging study using the MCF-7 cell line was conducted. For 2D imaging, the cells were cultivated with CND-P for 24 h, before testing. In contrast to previous reports based on CNDs obtained from citric acid and urea as precursors, the passivated CND-P was internalized by the cytoplasm, nucleolus, and nuclei of the MCF-7 cells [57]. To validate this occurrence, the cell’s nucleolus was stained with DAPI prior to imaging, which confirmed strong fluorescence emissions emanating from both the DAPI and CND-P in the overlay image (Fig. 7 ).

Images captured using 2D cell cultures (Fig. 7) are unable to confirm whether the fluorescent probes penetrated inside the cell or remained on the surface. To resolve this uncertainty, further tests were performed using 3D cell cultures to examine the internalization of CND-P in the cells. A cell culture media containing a final concentration of 50 µg/mL of CND-P used in the whole process of the 3D cell culture. The cells were cultured in RCCS vessels for three weeks, and the medium was replaced every three days until a cell aggregate was formed. From the 3D reconstructs (Fig. 8 ), we note that CND-P allows accurate 3D imaging of the MCF-7 cells and that the CND-P had penetrated into the cell cytoplasm and the nucleus as shown in Fig. 8(F–H). Thus, the 3D images confirm that the CND-P probes have been internalized by the cells rather than being bound or adsorbed on the surface of MCF-7 cells. These results suggest that the CND-P could serve as targeted drug delivery carrier and photothermal agent by reaching deep into the tumor cells for effective delivery of therapeutics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading, pH and two-photon triggered drug release with CND-P
ce:section.ce:para: The drug (DOX) loading capacity of CND-P was estimated using a standard DOX concentration curve generated in conjunction with the UV–vis spectrometer and a series of DOX solutions with variable concentrations. The loading capacity was estimated to be 0.98 mg/mg of CND-P, which agrees with results from previous studies [5]. Since the ζ-potential of DOX is +2.2 mV, and that of CND-P was measured to be -28.5 mV, the drug loading capacity of CND-P could be due to charge interactions between the carboxyl/amine groups of DOX and CND-P [58-60]. There was a small increase in ζ-potential observed for CND-P@DOX (−26.7 mV), indicating successful coupling of DOX with CND-P. Similar observations were noted by others [59-61]. The release profiles of DOX from CND-P@DOX at various pH levels in PBS are shown in Fig. S7 (ESI). These indicate that the amount of drug release increased at lower pH, because of the effect of growth protonation of -NH2 groups on DOX in an acidic medium. [5]. Thus, acidic microenvironments in endosomes, lysosomes, and tumors are suitable triggers for drug release in clinical chemotherapy.

To evaluate on-demand drug release of CND-P@DOX, five samples were irradiated for 5, 10, 15, 20 and 25 min by a 780 nm laser (35 mW). The interaction of light with the CND-P resulted in DOX release through the conformational changes that CND-P undergoes upon NIR irradiation as a function of time (5–25 min at 5 min interval). The drug release at the corresponding times was measured to be 14, 52, 61, 73 and 96wt%, respectively.

To verify the occurrence of on-demand drug release, a conical centrifuge filter tube (MWCO 3 kDa) was used to remove the released DOX from CND-P after irradiation. The released DOX passes through the filter while CND-P and CND-P@DOX remain inside. As can be seen from Fig. 9 A (absorption spectra of pure DOX and pure CND-P), both DOX and CND-P were excited using a 780 nm laser. However, as shown in Fig. 9B, the emission peak of CND-P and DOX are positioned at 530 and 590 nm, respectively. Thus, DOX release occurred after irradiation based on the emission peaks. Prior to drug release, the emission spectrum of the CND-P@DOX complex is shown in Fig. 9C, covering the range from 520 to 600 nm. After TPE, the emission peak position of the released DOX, which was separated by centrifugation (3 kDa-5000g), matches the emission peak position of pure DOX, confirming the release of the drug from CND-P (Fig. 9C). A standard DOX concentration curve was used to calculate the amount of released DOX from the CND-P after irradiation at variable times (Fig. 9D). After 25 min of irradiation, 96% DOX has been released. We note that control experiments without irradiation were performed to confirm the release behavior of CND-P@DOX. Our method confirms the effectiveness of upconverting capabilities of CND-P in drug delivery triggered by NIR I excitation as an external stimulus; thereby, precluding direct interactions between visible light and live cells, and the consequent damage to tissues.

To further investigate the real-time application of drug delivery via photoresponsive CND-P, we conducted photo-induced controlled drug release in a dynamic 3D cell culture environment. Controlled drug release was carried out by incubating CND-P with MCF-7 cells to validate the feasibility of using such a system. The amount of CND-P and DOX was chosen according to control cell viability experiments (Fig. 10 (A) and (B)), i.e., the cells were cultured with variable DOX concentrations (200, 500 1000 and 2000 nM) for 48 h and their cell viabilities were measured to determine the suitable concentration of DOX to apply. Since about 50% of the cells were destroyed at 1000 nM, it was chosen as the reference concentration for subsequent tests. As shown in Fig. 10(A), an increase in temperature was observed when solutions of CND-P (30, 40 and 50 μg/mL) were irradiated (780 nm, 35 mW laser). The variations in temperature upon irradiation of CND-P was measured in a highly controlled environment using a thermometer with a 0.1°C sensitivity [62]. After a 25 min irradiation, the media containing 50 μg/mL of CND-P generated more heat (T=50°C, ΔT=30°C) compared to the background energy observed for the control DI water (T=27°C, ΔT=7°C). Increasing the CND-P concentration beyond 50 μg/mL did not result in significant temperature rise, and the profiles reached a plateau after 25 min. Thus 50 μg/mL CND-P concentration was chosen for implementing photothermal therapy with enhanced heat generation. Because of the limited quantum yield of CND-P, most of the absorbed light is converted into heat through several nonradiative relaxation pathways [63]. This suggests the potential use of CND-P in photothermal therapy (PTT) [64]. When CND-P is excited at 780 nm, some of the incident photons are scattered by the nanoparticle while others are absorbed. The absorbed photons are responsible for heat production and luminescence. The total absorbed energy (i.e., the energy of the incident photons multiplied by the total number of absorbed photons) is released through a de-excitation processes that involve luminescence (the emission of photons at different energy levels from those of the incident photons) and non-radiative relaxation (generates heat). The relative contribution of any of these two de-excitation channels (heat and photoluminescence) is given by the fluorescence quantum yield [65].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multifunctional CND-P for two-photon mediated chemo-photothermal therapy
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the performance of CND-P@DOX nanocomplex as a multifunctional therapeutic agent, MCF-7 cells were seeded in three RCCS vessels and cultured for three weeks until a cell aggregate was formed. CND-P, DOX and CND-P@DOX were added to the cell culture media 24 h prior to laser irradiation (35 mW for 5, 15 and 25 min). Post irradiation, the cells were suspended in the media and incubated further for 48 h inside the RCCS vessels (please refer to Section 2.6 for more details). As shown in Fig. 11 , irradiation alone had a negligible effect on cell viability (control). With no laser irradiation (t=0), no heat was generated by CND-P, and no temperature change was observed. However, as the irradiation time increased, CND-P interaction with TPE generated heat. At 25 min, cell viability decreased to almost 65% due to the photothermal effect of CND-P. TPE had no significant effect on DOX efficiency, as the cell viability remained practically unchanged with irradiation time (similar to results in Fig. 10B at the same concentration). For CND-P@DOX, as the irradiation time increased, a greater proportion of DOX was released (Fig. 9D). After 25 min, about 96% of the drug was released and heat generated for photothermal therapy. Thus, the cumulative chemo- (DOX) and thermal therapy (CND-P) can destroy cancer cells with higher efficacy (20% viability), which is significantly greater than that of either CND-P or DOX alone with relative enhancements by factors of 2.6 and 1.8, respectively.

To further determine the colocalization of CND-P and DOX inside the cells, 3D cultured cells with CND-P@DOX nanocomplex were irradiated for 25 min and imaged after four hours of incubation. The 3D reconstructions of the cells (Fig. 12 ) clearly show that the emission signals from CND-P and dissociated DOX are detected at different locations in the MCF-7 cells, suggesting that the released drug penetrated the cell nucleolus. The DOX fluorescence signals were evenly distributed inside the cells owing to the intercellular release of DOX from CND-P.

Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) data given in Fig. 13 show photo-controlled DOX release in MCF-7 cells based on CND-P@DOX irradiation for 5, 15 and 25 min TPE (780 nm and 35 mW). A weak emission from DOX was detected in the cell with 5 min TPE exposure. By increasing the exposure time, more free DOX was detected in the cytoplasm. Clearly, a longer exposure time (15 and 25 min) resulted in continuous DOX release and stronger DOX fluorescence signals inside the cells. Another contributing factor is the change in temperature that affects the ζ-potential. For CND-P, the measured ζ-potential at ambient temperature and after 25 min irradiation (∼5°C) was −28.5 mV and −22 mV, respectively. The changes in ζ-potential resulted in further dissociation of DOX from CND-P and a stronger emission signal.

We note that no signal from CND-P or DOX could be detected in the cell culture media in different z-planes, suggesting that all the nanoparticles and drug were internalized by the cells. Video clips of the CLSM (z-stack imaging) observations showing CND-P fluorescence (compared to DAPI) and DOX release from CND-P@DOX into MCF-7 cells after TPE are given in the ESI (Mov. S1, S2) showing CND-P and DOX in different z-planes of MCF-7 breast cancer cells.

We synthesized a photoresponsive nanoparticle (CND-P@DOX) for the destruction of breast cancer cells (MCF-7) by remotely initiating the drug release and photothermal therapy via two-photon excitation. A facile method was devised for synthesizing CND-P having 53% quantum yield which corresponds to QY enhancements by factors of thirteen and five over those obtained from citric acid alone and in combination urea, respectively. The key mechanisms underpinning emissions (from the carbogenic core and surface defects of CNDs) by NIR two-photon excitation were characterized. The drug loading capacity of CND-P was measured as 0.98 w/w with the ability to transport and release DOX into MCF-7 cells via two-photon excitation. Due to the heat generation capability of CND-P, MCF-7 cell apoptosis was enhanced, and the power of DOX in inducing cell death was increased through the combined effect of DOX and photothermal effect of CND-P. In particular, 3D cultured cell viability results showed that the combined chemo-photothermal treatment had destroyed cancer cells in significantly greater proportion than that of CND-P or DOX alone with relative enhancements by factors of 2.6 and 1.8, respectively. A promising aspect of this work is the possibility to harness two-photon triggered drug delivery in conjunction with CND-P loaded with appropriate drugs. Our advanced strategy of combined chemo- and photothermal therapy via two-photon excitation to destroy MCF-7 cells with no damage to hemoglobin, myoglobin and melanin cells could be an effective approach for other cancer cell treatments.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the support of this work by the Australian Postgraduate Award (SMA and AD). The authors thank Dr. Lai and Dr. Hua at the University of Sydney with biology based experiments and Dr. Young and Dr. Tjin of ACMM for their assistance with imaging.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2017.07.165.
